abstract.text: This article describes the essential parts of KeyboardKit.

inlineContent.text: KeyboardKit extends Apple’s native APIs and provides you with a lot more functionality. It lets you mimic the native iOS keyboard and tweak its style and behavior, or create completely custom keyboards.
KeyboardKit also has a
view that mimics the native iOS keyboard and can be customized & styled to great extent. Many of the tools and views in the SDK are used by the system keyboard.

unlocks a lot of essential Pro features, such as a toggle toolbar, a system keyboard input toolbar, additional system keyboard views, previews, etc. Information about Pro features can be found at the end of this article.
KeyboardKit uses namespaces to make the API surface smaller, by nesting types in logical groups. By typing
and
, Xcode will provide you with a list of all types in this essential namespace.
KeyboardKit has other namespaces as well, that extend the library with more specific capabilities, like
, etc.
The
namespace has a lot of essential types, like
, etc. that are essential when for creating a custom keyboard. It also contains common views, styles, etc.
Namespaces will not contain protocols, nor important types that are meant to be exposed as top-level types. This includes observable context and settings types, the
and important components like the
is the most essential type in the library. Just make your
KeyboardController
inherit this class to get access to a bunch of additional capabilities and view lifecycle functions.
The
defines shared
properties, and
, that can be used to avoid relying on the controller within your code.
KeyboardKit also has a
protocol that aims to make it easier to use KeyboardKit in platforms that don’t support UIKit. This is however not fully implemented yet, although many types use it.
KeyboardKit has a
class that provides observable keyboard state that keeps the keyboard UI up to date with its current state. It has a
reference, lets you get and set
, etc.
You can use this to update the keyboard. For instance, setting the
will update the
accordingly.
Other namespaces have their own context types, like
, etc. They will all automatically update the keyboard, provided that the keyboard view observes them,
KeyboardKit automatically creates instances of these classes and injects them into
, and syncs with the controller when needed.
KeyboardKit has an
class that provides observable keyboard settings that are automatically persisted when they are changed. It has a
property and will be extended with more settings over time.
Other namespaces have their own setting types, like
, etc. KeyboardKit will observe any changes made to these types and update the keyboard accordingly.
KeyboardKit automatically creates instances of these classes, injects them into
, and syncs with the controller when needed.
KeyboardKit has a
protocol that can be used to define the keyboard’s behavior. It’s used by some services, like the standard
, to make behavior-based decisions.
KeyboardKit automatically creates an instance of
and injects it into
. You can replace it at any time, by implementing the procotol from scratch or by inheriting and customizing the standard behavior.
The
article describes how you can use a
to style your keyboard in a dynamic and flexible way. Various views have their own styles as well, that can be applied with view modifiers.

The keyboard appearance model can be used to define if a keyboard is light or dark. This is not the same thing as the color scheme. A keyboard can be defined as “dark” even in light mode, and will render slightly darker than the default appearance.
KeyboardKit has an
that can be applied with the
view modifier:
Note that applying a dark appearances will make iOS tell the extension that the
color scheme
is dark, while this may in fact not be true. This will lead to visual bugs, that require workarounds. See the
article for more information.

KeyboardKit extends native types with a lot more information, to make it easier to implement features like autocomplete, text analysis, etc. Check the extension section in the bottom of the documentation root, or the
folder for more info.
For instance, KeyboardKit extends types in
, and other platform-specific frameworks.
KeyboardKit has a

that mimics a native iOS keyboard. It can be used for alphabetic, numeric & symbolic keyboards, supports all supported locales, layouts, callouts, etc., and can be styled to great extent with styles & themes:
can be customized to great extent. You can pass in custom services & state, replace any part of the keyboard, and enable additional components:

code: controller: controller,             // You can setup the view with a controller instance
buttonContent: { $0.view },         // Can be used to customize the content view of any button
buttonView: { $0.view },            // Can be used to customize the entire view of any button
emojiKeyboard: { $0.view },         // Can be used to customize the emoji keyboard, if any
toolbar: { params in params.view }  // Can be used to customize the toolbar above the keyboard

inlineContent.text: To use the standard views, just return
, or
. Otherwise, just return the view you want to use for the provided parameters, which contain contextual information.
KeyboardKit also provides gerneral, keyboard-specific views in the
namespace, which means that you need to prefix the names below with
when using them (this applies to all namespaces in the library):
KeyboardKit has a collection of keyboard
views and styles that can be used to mimic all parts of a native keyboard, as well as their gestures. The
renders the full button, while other views like
renders parts of it.
Most of the views can be styled with a
, which can be applied with the
view modifier. This is however not yet true for the
itself, which uses a
to support more complex styling.
KeyboardKit has a
that integrates with the native keyboard switcher, to select the next keyboard on tap and show a menu with all available keyboards on long press.
KeyboardKit will by default map the
action to this view, and automatically register the required
on launch. You just have to add a
button, and it will just work.
KeyboardKit has a keyboard
that applies a minimum height to its content. It can be used to stop input & action callouts from being cut off, since a custom iOS keyboard can’t render outside of its frame.
This view can be styled with a
, which can be applied with the
view modifier:
KeyboardKit uses style view modifiers to great extent. This lets you style most KeyboardKit views just like regular SwiftUI views, like how a SwiftUI
can be styled with
This is however not (yet) true for more complex views, like
, where KeyboardKit instead uses the
concept to provide dynamic styles to any part of the view hieararchy.
See the
article for more information about KeyboardKit view styling.
unlocks additional, powerful
capabilities, including full support for all
s, a full-blown
, input toolbars, powerful
s, etc.
KeyboardKit Pro unlocks additional
-related views and utils, that make it easier to create more powerful keyboards:
KeyboardKit Pro unlocks a
component that can be used to just render a system keyboard bottom row that by default uses the same layout configuration as the full system keyboard.
This view is convenient if you want to replace the system keyboard with another view, but keep a bottom button row with some actions.
KeyboardKit Pro unlocks an
, which is automatically added to
when a valid license is registered.
The keyboard has support for categories, runtime version adjustments and skintones. Read more about this in the
article.
KeyboardKit Pro will automatically add an input toolbar to
if
is applied to the view hierarchy, with an
or
configuration.
The toolbar is added between the autocomplete toolbar and the system keyboard, and by default uses a slightly smaller button height.
KeyboardKit Pro unlocks localized services for all supported
s, which lets you create fully localized keyboards.
The various license tiers unlock different amount of
s, where Gold unlocks all. Read more in the
article.
KeyboardKit Pro unlocks a
, which can be used to preview various configurations, locales, themes, etc.
This view can also be used to show keyboard configurations to your users, e.g. on a settings screen. Read more in the
article.
KeyboardKit Pro also unlocks more general keyboard views in the
namespace, to let you create more complex keyboards:
The
can be used to toggle between two toolbars, e.g. to place a main menu “behind” the autocomplete toolbar:
This view wraps itself in a
, which means that it can also be styled with the
modifier.
